skill.name: open-show
skill.description: Convert Markdown, Word, PDF, text, local HTML, or web pages into a single fullscreen HTML slideshow.
skill.summary: Create offline playable HTML slide decks from common document and web inputs.
skill.author: lumacoder

security_audit.summary: Static command and weak-cryptography alerts in README.md and SKILL.md are mostly documentation or false positives, but the Python converter has confirmed high-risk behavior. Untrusted HTML, Markdown, DOCX text, TXT text, and URL content can be written into executable HTML, and local image inlining can read arbitrary local files referenced by untrusted HTML.

high_findings.title: Local File Disclosure Through Image Inlining
high_findings.description: Local HTML image sources are resolved against the input path, then fall back to absolute or relative filesystem paths. If an untrusted HTML file references a sensitive local path, the converter can read that file and embed it into the output as a data URI.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file read is direct and the fallback Path(src) permits absolute paths. Exploitation requires processing attacker-controlled local HTML or Markdown that contains a crafted image source.

high_findings.title: Generated Deck Preserves Untrusted Executable Markup
high_findings.description: The converter preserves HTML nodes and inner content from input documents, then writes them into a browser-executable HTML deck. Script tags are removed, but event handlers, dangerous attributes, and unescaped text can still become active content when the generated file is opened.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code uses str(node) and raw inner content instead of an allowlist sanitizer. The generated output includes active JavaScript support, so browser execution is a realistic impact.

medium_findings.title: Unrestricted URL and Image Fetching
medium_findings.description: The script fetches user-supplied URLs and remote image URLs without host allowlisting or private-network blocking. This is expected for a web-to-slides tool, but it can be abused for server-side request forgery in automated environments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network fetching is explicit and input-controlled. The risk depends on where the skill runs and whether it can reach internal services.

medium_findings.title: TLS Certificate Verification Disabled on Retry
medium_findings.description: When a URL fetch fails, the script retries with certificate verification disabled. This can allow a network attacker to modify fetched content that later becomes part of the generated deck.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The verify=False argument is explicit. The impact is limited to URL conversion paths but can affect generated presentation content.

low_findings.title: Documented Shell Commands Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: Many static external-command findings are fenced command examples in README.md and SKILL.md for dependency checks, installation, and CLI usage. They are not hidden execution paths, but they do instruct users to run pip install commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visible markdown command examples. They still matter operationally because users may execute them during setup.

low_findings.title: Fixed External Command Launch Has Low Injection Risk
low_findings.description: The optional OpenClaw launch uses subprocess.run with a fixed argument list and a generated file URL. There is no shell=True usage or direct shell interpolation at the cited location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call uses a list of arguments and does not invoke a shell. It still launches an external program, so it remains a low operational risk.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography alerts point to Markdown prose, CSS layout code, JavaScript slide navigation, and file-writing logic rather than cryptographic algorithms. No evidence of MD5, SHA1, or custom cryptography was found in the reviewed locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected code at these locations performs pagination, rendering, or documentation. I did not find cryptographic operations in those cited blocks.

dangerous_patterns.title: Raw HTML Assembly from Parsed Input
dangerous_patterns.description: Parsed input is converted with raw node strings and inserted into a generated HTML document without a strict sanitizer.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The construction path is clear from parser output to final HTML. Exact exploitability depends on the input format and browser behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Read During Image Embedding
dangerous_patterns.description: Image source handling can open local files and embed their bytes into output HTML.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code opens the resolved local path directly and base64-encodes its contents. The path can come from input markup.

dangerous_patterns.title: HTTPS Retry Without Certificate Verification
dangerous_patterns.description: The URL parser retries failed requests with TLS certificate verification disabled.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The verify=False call is explicit and is reached after the initial request exception. This is a known insecure transport pattern.

content.user_title: Convert Documents into HTML Slide Decks
content.value_statement: Preparing slides from existing documents takes manual formatting and layout work. OpenShow converts files or web pages into a single playable HTML deck with navigation and a timer.
actual_capabilities: Converts Markdown, DOCX, PDF, TXT, local HTML, and URL inputs into HTML slide decks.
Builds a single HTML output with inline CSS, JavaScript, images, and logo markup.
Splits content into slides using headings, text length, image count, and block count.
Chooses cover, text, list, split, image, and closing layouts based on slide content.
Adds keyboard controls, click zones, touch navigation, fullscreen support, and a timer.
Can optionally open the generated deck in a browser or OpenClaw after conversion.
limitations: It does not import PPTX files or older DOC files.
Large PDFs can produce very large HTML files because pages become embedded images.
Complex web pages may lose content during heuristic main article extraction.
Untrusted HTML, Markdown, or URLs should be reviewed before opening generated decks.

use_cases.title: Turn Research Notes into a Deck
use_cases.description: Convert Markdown or text notes into a navigable presentation for review meetings.

use_cases.title: Present a Report Without Rebuilding Slides
use_cases.description: Transform a DOCX or PDF report into a fullscreen HTML deck for team updates.

use_cases.title: Share Web Articles as Presentations
use_cases.description: Convert a web page into a portable HTML slideshow for teaching or demos.

prompt_templates.title: Convert a Local File
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner conversion from a document path.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use OpenShow to convert this Markdown file into a playable HTML slideshow: ./notes.md

prompt_templates.title: Create a Deck from a URL
prompt_templates.scenario: Convert an article while preserving main content.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use OpenShow to turn this article URL into an offline HTML slide deck: https://example.com/article

prompt_templates.title: Convert and Open the Result
prompt_templates.scenario: Generate output and launch it for review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert ./quarterly-report.docx into an HTML slideshow and open it when finished.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Review Deck Safely
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow with security review for untrusted input.
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this saved web page into a slide deck, then summarize any remote images or active markup that may affect offline review.

output_examples.input: A Markdown project brief with headings, bullet lists, and screenshots.
output_examples.output: A fullscreen HTML deck with a cover slide, section slides, list slides, embedded images, page progress, and timer controls.

output_examples.input: A PDF handout for a classroom lecture.
output_examples.output: A single HTML presentation where each PDF page appears as an image slide with keyboard and touch navigation.

output_examples.input: A web article URL containing text and inline images.
output_examples.output: A cleaned article-based slide deck with the main content extracted and images embedded when downloads succeed.

best_practices: Use trusted input files, especially when converting HTML, Markdown, or web pages.
Review the generated deck before presenting because automatic pagination can split content unexpectedly.
Keep PDFs reasonably small when the deck must be emailed or opened on mobile devices.
anti_patterns: Do not convert untrusted HTML and immediately open the output in a privileged browser session.
Do not use this skill as a secure sanitizer for hostile web pages or documents.
Do not expect precise slide design control from the automatic layout engine.

faq.question: Does OpenShow create PPTX files?
faq.answer: No. It creates a single HTML presentation file, not a PowerPoint file.

faq.question: Can the output work offline?
faq.answer: Yes, the generated deck inlines CSS, JavaScript, and many images for offline playback.

faq.question: Which inputs are supported?
faq.answer: It supports Markdown, DOCX, PDF, TXT, local HTML, and HTTP or HTTPS URLs.

faq.question: Does it need external CDNs?
faq.answer: No. The slide engine writes CSS and JavaScript into the generated HTML file.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted web pages?
faq.answer: Use caution. The converter preserves some markup and can fetch remote resources.

faq.question: Can it open the deck automatically?
faq.answer: Yes. The script supports opening the result with the system browser or OpenClaw.
